description: You are a network engineer who has collected three ICMP-echo (ping) diagnostic files from a colleague. Your goal is to build an isolated Python 3 virtual environment with venv, install the ping3 package, create a tiny parser script that analyses the three ping dumps, and finally generate a CSV-style connectivity report. Do NOT run any real network pings – everything must be produced from the supplied text files. All artefacts must live inside your home directory (/home/user) so that automated grading can find them. Required end-state 1. Directory structure • /home/user/net_tshoot/ – project root you create • /home/user/net_tshoot/env/ – the virtual-environment directory generated with python3 -m venv • /home/user/net_tshoot/analyze_connectivity.py – the script you will write (see exact specification below) • /home/user/net_tshoot/requirements.txt – produced with pip freeze > from inside the venv • /home/user/netlogs/ – log directory (create it if missing) • /home/user/netlogs/connectivity_report.log – the final report produced by running your script once 2. Virtual environment requirements • Must be built with the standard library’s venv module. • The third-party package ping3 (any available version) must be installed **inside** the venv. • The venv’s pip freeze output must be saved verbatim to /home/user/net_tshoot/requirements.txt. 3. Input ping dump files (already present – do NOT modify) • /home/user/network_dumps/ping_8.8.8.8.log • /home/user/network_dumps/ping_1.1.1.1.log • /home/user/network_dumps/ping_example.com.log 4. Script behaviour (analyze_connectivity.py) a) Parse **only** the three files above. b) For each file, determine: • HOST – derived from the filename (e.g. ping_8.8.8.8.log → 8.8.8.8). • STATUS – “UP” if at least one line contains the substring "time="; otherwise “DOWN”. • RTT_ms – the first RTT value found after "time=", rounded to one decimal place; if host is DOWN, use “NA”. c) Count totals: TOTAL_UP and TOTAL_DOWN. d) Write a **four-line** log file at /home/user/netlogs/connectivity_report.log with this exact CSV-style layout (one line per item, no extra spaces): HOST,STATUS,RTT_ms HOST,STATUS,RTT_ms HOST,STATUS,RTT_ms SUMMARY,TOTAL_UP=<X>,TOTAL_DOWN=<Y> Example (values will differ): 8.8.8.8,UP,14.2 1.1.1.1,UP,12.7 example.com,DOWN,NA SUMMARY,TOTAL_UP=2,TOTAL_DOWN=1 e) When the script is run, it should **only** print the absolute path of the log file it generated (no other stdout). 5. Verification hooks (strictly enforced) • The file /home/user/netlogs/connectivity_report.log must exist and contain exactly 4 non-empty lines following the format above. • The venv directory must include a pyvenv.cfg file and a bin/python interpreter. • ping3 must appear in /home/user/net_tshoot/requirements.txt. • Running the script a second time must overwrite (not append to) the log file with the same four-line structure. Hints (do not reveal to verification): • You can parse RTT with a simple split("time=")[1].split()[0] approach. • Remember to activate the venv before pip install. • Use exactly the filenames provided; automated tests are case-sensitive. This is a multi-step task: set up the environment, install packages, write and execute the parser, and verify the generated artefacts. Good luck!

File and directory permissions preset before start:
• /home/user/network_dumps – 755  
• individual .log files – 644  
• /home/user/netlogs – 755 (empty)  

What the grader will check:
1. /home/user/net_tshoot/env/pyvenv.cfg exists.  
2. /home/user/net_tshoot/requirements.txt contains a line that begins with “ping3==”.  
3. /home/user/netlogs/connectivity_report.log exists, has 4 lines, and its content exactly matches the block above.  
4. The script’s stdout, when executed a second time, is precisely “/home/user/netlogs/connectivity_report.log\n”.

The grader will execute:
/home/user/net_tshoot/env/bin/python /home/user/net_tshoot/analyze_connectivity.py
